explanation: The specification is describing a condition that should hold specifically at the end of each inspiration phase, but because 'inspiration phase' boundaries are not specified as atomic propositions and we're only given prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2, the LTL can only depict that at some moment (such as at the end of a phase, assumed as a relevant state), 'prop_0 and prop_1 and prop_2' must all hold. Thus, the formula 'F (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2)' expresses that eventually there is a state in which all three hold simultaneously, which is the closest LTL translation given the atomic propositions provided and the description.

The requirement states that at the end of an inspiration phase, if both prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 should occur. Without further detail on modeling the 'end of inspiration phase', we interpret 'at the end' as: whenever it becomes true that an inspiration phase ends and (prop_0 & prop_1) holds, then prop_2 must be true at that time. Assuming no explicit 'end' atomic, a common approach is to say that whenever (prop_0 & prop_1) becomes true (possibly across steps), prop_2 should hold then.

The specification says: 'At the prop_0 if prop_1 and the prop_2, a prop_3.' Interpreting this, whenever prop_0 holds, if prop_1 and prop_2 hold at that moment, then prop_3 should also hold at that moment. This translates to: globally, at any time, if prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2, then prop_3.

The specification states that at the end of an inspiration phase, if prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 must also hold. Assuming 'the end of an inspiration phase' is not an atomic proposition we can reference directly, we must express this as a safety condition where whenever (prop_0 & prop_1) holds at any transition point (i.e., any step), then prop_2 must also hold at that same step.

The requirement states that at the end of an inspiration phase, if 'prop_0' is false and 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_2' must happen. Assuming the 'end of inspiration' is an implicit trigger for this rule (not expressed as an atomic proposition), the LTL translation uses the 'globally' operator to specify that whenever (!prop_0 & prop_1) holds, then prop_2 must also hold at that moment (since there's no explicit timing shift indicated).

This requirement specifies that, at the end of every inspiration phase, if both prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 must also hold at that moment. Assuming 'end of inspiration phase' is characterized by a specific instant (modeled implicitly by using the conjunction at every time step), we capture this as: it is always the case that if prop_0 and prop_1 are true, then prop_2 is also true at the same time.

The statement says that at the occurrence of prop_0, if both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_3 should also hold at that time. In LTL, this is naturally captured by stating that globally, whenever prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2 holds, prop_3 must also hold.

The natural language requires that at the end of each inspiration phase, if both prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 must also hold at that time. Since the timing for 'end of an inspiration phase' is not defined with an atomic proposition, we interpret the requirement as 'always: if prop_0 and prop_1, then prop_2'.

The statement requires that at the end of each inspiration phase, if both prop_0 and prop_1 are true, then prop_2 should hold. Since 'at the end of inspiration phase' is not formally defined, we interpret it as: whenever a moment occurs that is right after the last moment where both prop_0 and prop_1 hold (i.e., at the border where (prop_0 & prop_1) stops being true), then prop_2 should occur at that moment. In LTL, such a trigger can be captured as: whenever X(!(prop_0 & prop_1)) & (prop_0 & prop_1), then in the next state prop_2 must hold.
